content.text: Read _plan.md and do the P4 kickoff brief

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by locating and reading `_Plan.md`, since it's outside the repo in a parent folder.

content.content: Northwestern Project\_Plan.md
No files found

1	# GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge — Analysis Plan
2	
3	**Status:** v2.1 (2026-07-04). v1 (2026-07-03) was QA'd by five independent cold-reader agents, each scoring it against one part of the competition brief (the four Stage-2 dimensions plus deliverables/Stage-1) with **no access to the Market Trends sample process**; v2 folded in their findings. v2.1 locks four decisions: **deadline 2026-07-15**, pilot corpus (§2), overall-submission scoring assumption (§1), and repo layout (§4). Raw data still downloading. Working repo: `gain-investigation/` (this folder's sibling).
4	
5	**Update 2026-07-06:** §9 (new) houses a **story-bucket-driven tool roadmap** from teammate (Chris) input — the three story buckets the tools should serve, the three data-integrity notes that gate accuracy, and a prioritized P1–P7 tool list. Note also that since v2.1 the full corpus has been built (`db/lda_full.duckdb`), the ledger has been reset to a fresh full-corpus pass (leads L020–L027), and `emergence_and_flows.sql` / `press_issue_coupling.sql` are now the working lenses; §2–§7 below still read as of 2026-07-04 and have not been retrofitted.
6	
7	**Standing rule adopted from QA:** every submitted artifact (SKILL.md files, findings report, README, traces index) must stand alone for an evaluator — **no references to the Market Trends process or its jargon**. Lineage notes live only in Appendix A of this internal plan.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## 1. What we must produce (deliverables)
12	
13	1. **Agent Skill(s)** — per the Agent Skills spec (SKILL.md + YAML frontmatter, optional `scripts/`, `references/`, `assets/`). Must validate against the spec and be **re-runnable by an evaluator**: "if an evaluator can't re-run your skill, it cannot score above 1 on any dimension."
14	2. **Findings report** — newsworthy discoveries sourced to specific records. "A polished summary of what the dataset contains isn't a finding; something a reporter would chase is."
15	3. **Interaction traces** — full session logs (raw JSON or rendered page) showing inputs, tool calls, outputs, and **the moments where human judgment intervened**, keyed to skill invocations.
16	4. **README.md** — six required elements: included skills; which findings each supports; where the relevant traces are; any outside data used; any conflicts of interest; whether findings suggest possible legal violations.
17	
18	**Judging.** Stage 1 verifies findings (accuracy, sourcing, genuine public interest) — submissions failing it get no further ranking. Stage 2 scores skills 0–3 on: Investigation Organization, Corpus Efficiency, Human Verifiability, Extended Capability.
19	
20	**Scoring unit (decided 2026-07-04).** Team assumption: scoring is on the **overall submission and the findings it generates**, not per skill. Consequence: the five-skill structure stands without merge pressure. Every skill still ships runnable scripts — the re-runnability threshold applies regardless of scoring unit.
21	
22	**Deadline: 2026-07-15** (11 days from v2.1). Timeboxes in §7. The MVS in §2 is the floor; anything at risk of missing it gets cut, not compressed.
23	
24	---
25	
26	## 2. Strategy: findings first
27	
28	Stage 1 is a hard gate; infrastructure only matters if we surface genuinely newsworthy findings. Three commitments:
29	
30	- **Minimum viable submission (MVS):** 2–3 verified findings naming specific actors, dates, amounts, and records + `lda-corpus-loader` + `finding-verifier` + indexed traces + complete README. Everything beyond that is upside, cut first when time is short.
31	- **Tracer bullet before build-out:** before full entity resolution or all seven lenses, run ONE lens end-to-end on ONE slice (e.g., 2025 Senate filings + 2025 press releases): ETL the slice → SQL scan → deep-read → draft finding → verification pass → trace captured and indexed. This proves the pipeline, surfaces schema surprises, calibrates token costs, and produces a candidate finding in week one.
32	- **Named-actor rule:** any lead or finding that names no specific actor, date, and record ID is auto-rejected at triage. "Spend on issue X spiked" is a dataset summary (explicitly disqualified); "Client Y tripled spend on issue X the quarter before Senator Z's markup, and Y's lobbyist is Z's former chief of staff, per filings A/B and release C" is a finding.
33	
34	**Pilot corpus (decided 2026-07-04): 2025 full year + 2026 Q1, all three datasets.** All skills are built and the full workflow (lenses → triage → deep dive → verification → traces) is exercised on this slice first; the full corpus is loaded only where verified leads demand history. Why a time slice and not a person/issue subset:
35	
36	- **Time slices are free; entity slices are not.** The corpus is partitioned by time on disk (press: year dirs; Senate: per-year JSON files; House: per-year-quarter dirs). Selecting 2025+2026-Q1 means pointing the loader at ~6 of 20+ partitions — zero filtering code. Filtering by person or issue requires parsing all 800K+ files *first* to know which ones match, which is the full-corpus ETL we're trying to defer.
37	- **Ground truth for sanity checks.** The data manual publishes 2025 counts for every dataset (~48K releases, 108,225 Senate filings, ~108K House records) — the only year we can reconcile the ETL against published numbers.
38	- **Five quarters supports the temporal lenses** (Q-over-Q spend spikes, say-vs-pay windows), and ending at 2026-Q1 keeps findings current — recent findings are more newsworthy. If a lead needs a YoY baseline or deeper history, adding 2024 (or any year) is one `--years` flag, not new code.
39	- **Roughly 25% of the corpus** (~110K files, ~2 GB) — real enough to hit schema surprises, small enough to iterate in minutes.
40	- **Narrowing to people/issues happens *after* load, in SQL** — once the pilot DB exists, "only HCR-code filings" or "only Senator X" is a WHERE clause, not a data-prep step.
41	
42	Caveat on the lens list (§3 Layer 3): it starts from the data manual's own "starting points," which **every competitor has**. Differentiation comes from (a) the crosswalks that make cross-dataset lenses actually executable (entity resolution, `bioguide_id` joins), (b) named-actor depth, and (c) verification rigor. Expect the best findings at lens *intersections* (revolving door × say-vs-pay).
43	
44	---
45	
46	## 3. Architecture — three layers
47	
48	### Layer 1 — Deterministic data foundation (pure Python, no LLM)
49	
50	ETL scripts (`scripts/build_db.py` + helpers) parse all three datasets into one **DuckDB** database (engine provisional — see Decision Register §8):
51	
52	- `press_releases` — 51 JSONL files (504 MB): url, title, date, bioguide_id, name, party, state, chamber, text, plus `src_file`, `src_line`.
53	- `senate_filings`, `senate_activities`, `senate_lobbyists`, `senate_contributions`, `senate_contribution_items` — flattened from nested JSON (2.2 GB), **streamed with ijson** (year files are hundreds of MB). Keyed by `filing_uuid`; every row keeps source path + array index.
54	- `house_filings`, `house_lobbyists`, `house_issues` — streaming parse of 409,650 XML files (5.9 GB). Keyed by XML filename (House filing ID); `senateID`/`houseID` kept for the crosswalk.
55	- `members` — distinct member metadata keyed by `bioguide_id`.
56	
57	**Pointer rule:** every table row carries a raw-record pointer (source file + line/index or filename) so any query result resolves to a citable raw record in one step.
58	
59	**Press-text enrichment (named component).** The press corpus is the only unstructured dataset (~50M+ words) and feeds the flagship say-vs-pay lens. Deterministic-first pipeline:
60	1. spaCy NER (orgs, people) + regex (bill numbers `H.R. 1234`/`S. 567`) + keyword lexicon mapped to ALI issue codes → materialized once into `press_entities` / `press_topics` tables.
61	2. Cheap-model batch (Haiku) only for residual classification on SQL-filtered slices — never the full corpus.
62	3. Frontier-model reading only for records already attached to a lead.
63	Measure cost/accuracy on a one-month sample before the full run.
64	
65	**`scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>`** — prints any raw record (Senate filing by `filing_uuid`, House filing by XML ID, press release by `src_file:src_line`) in one command, straight from the raw files. This is both the evaluator's fast-review path and the **only** way agents access raw records — no agent greps or opens raw corpus files directly.
66	
67	**Resolution + aggregates:** `entities` + `entity_aliases` (normalized names, Senate↔House crosswalk via shared `senateID`/`houseID` validated by fuzzy name match); spend per client/issue/quarter; releases per member/month (the manual's member-activity baselines); `covered_position` extraction; **`bill_mentions`** — bill numbers (`H.R. 1234` / `S. 567`) regexed from House `specific_issues` free text, Senate activity descriptions, AND press-release text into one table keyed by record pointer. That's the manual's bill-level lobbying map, and the highest-precision cross-dataset join key we have (a bill number is unambiguous where an org name is not). Plus a **sanity report** reconciling row counts against the manual's stated scale (~108K Senate filings 2025, ~48K releases 2025) before anything downstream is trusted.
68	
69	**Citation rules:**
70	- Record-level claims cite raw records via citation keys. The database is never the cited source.
71	- **Aggregate claims** (spikes, rankings, counts) cite three things: the exact SQL, the one-command DB rebuild (`build_db.py`), and ≥3 sampled underlying raw records shown via `show_record.py`. That is how an evaluator verifies an aggregate without trusting us.
72	- Press releases cite the corpus record (`src_file:src_line`) as primary, live URL secondary (URLs rot; the scraped text is the evidence).
73	
74	### Layer 2 — Investigation ledger + decision log
75	
76	Two files at repo root, maintained across every session. This is the graded Organization artifact, so the schema is fixed here, not improvised later.
77	
78	**`LEDGER.md`** — four sections:
79	
80	1. **Leads** (table): `id | hypothesis (one line) | lens | named actors | status | owner | evidence so far (record IDs) | next action | updated`
81	2. **Entities checked**: entity → verdict → record IDs examined (linked to the Layer-1 `entities` table by entity id, so "checked" has a precise referent)
82	3. **Queries run**: SQL + one-line result + date (reusable, prevents re-treading)
83	4. **Cold threads**: parked leads with reason + revisit trigger
84	
85	**Status state machine:** `open → triaged → investigating → verified | dead`; any lead may move to `parked` (cold) with a reason. **Cold ≠ dead:** dead = affirmatively refuted or judged not newsworthy (reason recorded); cold = promising but blocked or deprioritized, reconsidered at every triage checkpoint.
86	
87	**Write protocol (single-writer):** sub-agents never edit the ledger — they return structured candidate rows; only the orchestrating session (or a human) commits them. Update moments: session start (read), any status change, session end (write). With multiple teammates, the ledger lives in git; the owner column arbitrates.
88	
89	**Mid-stream leads:** the brief explicitly scores "new leads that arrive mid-stream." The Phase-3 lock applies to the *investigation slate*, not the ledger: leads discovered during deep dives enter the ledger as `open` and are considered at the next triage checkpoint (held at each phase boundary, or ad hoc if a lead looks hot). Nothing is lost; the slate stays deliberate.
90	
91	**`DECISIONS.md`** — human-judgment log, required because traces must show "moments where human judgment intervened": every intervention (triage selections, lead kills, editorial calls, legal-flag decisions) logged as `date | decision | rationale | who | trace file`. Referenced from the README and from each finding's verification block.
92	
93	### Layer 3 — Lenses (lead generation)
94	
95	Each lens starts with SQL against Layer 1 (deterministic, cheap), surfaces anomalies, then samples raw records for LLM reading. Scanner output = candidate ledger rows (record IDs + one-line hypotheses), never quoted evidence.
96	
97	1. **Say vs. pay** — member press-release topics vs lobbying spend targeting their chamber/committee in the same quarter (`bioguide_id` + press enrichment + `bill_mentions`). Chamber-level works from internal data (`government_entities` names the chambers); committee-level needs member committee assignments — if this lens makes the slate, pull the lightweight congress-legislators committee-membership data in Phase 1 (external, disclosed) rather than discovering the gap mid-dive.
98	2. **Revolving door** — `covered_position` mining ("Chief of Staff to Sen. …") → who now lobbies their old office, on what
99	3. **Anomaly scan (money + messaging)** — quarter-over-quarter spend spikes per client/issue and sudden appearances/disappearances; plus the press side: member release-rate outliers vs their own baseline, and issue-term frequency shifts timed against spend spikes (the manual's temporal lobbying–messaging coupling lead)
100	4. **Senate↔House discrepancies** — same engagement (shared `senateID`), different reported income/issues
101	5. **Contribution flows** — LD-203 `payee`/`honoree` networks; FEC ground-truthing
102	6. **Foreign influence** — `foreign_entities[]` cross-referenced with FARA
103	7. **Data-quality stories** — systematic disclosure gaps (missing income, late filings) — must still resolve to named filers and specific records per the named-actor rule
104	
105	**Parked deliberately (from the manual's press starting points):** comparative messaging — clustering same-event releases in a ±7-day window to contrast party framing. It's a real technique but weak for this brief in 11 days: its natural output is a messaging-patterns story (close to the "polished summary" Stage 1 disqualifies) rather than a named-actor pay-to-shape-policy finding, and event-window clustering is real engineering. Revisit only if a slated lead needs framing-shift evidence, in which case run it narrowly on that lead's event window.
106	
107	**Model-tier and budget rules** (the Corpus Efficiency dimension scores exactly this):
108	
109	| Work | Tier |
110	|---|---|
111	| ETL, SQL, NER, regex, indexes | no LLM |
112	| Press-topic residual classification | Haiku (batch, filtered slices only) |
113	| Lens scanning → candidate lead rows | Haiku/Sonnet; returns record IDs + one-liners only |
114	| Deep-read of lead-attached records; finding drafting | frontier |
115	| Finding verification (re-derivation) | frontier, fresh session |
116	
117	Hard rules: agents access raw records only via `show_record.py`; deep-read sessions cap at ~30 records; scanners never quote record text into the orchestrator's context.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## 4. Skills + re-runnability contract
122	
123	Candidate skills with explicit I/O contracts (final count and boundaries decided at packaging — §8):
124	
125	| Skill | Inputs | Outputs | Prereq | One-command demo |
126	|---|---|---|---|---|
127	| `lda-corpus-loader` | `DATA_ROOT` (layout per data manual) | `lda.duckdb` + sanity report | — | `python scripts/build_db.py --data-root <path> --sample 2025-Q1` (smoke mode: minutes, not hours) |
128	| `lda-entity-resolver` | `lda.duckdb` | `entities`/`entity_aliases` tables + crosswalk QA report | loader | `python scripts/resolve_entities.py` |
129	| `investigation-ledger` | — | `LEDGER.md`/`DECISIONS.md` templates + `scripts/ledger_lint.py` (schema check an evaluator can run) | — | `python scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md` |
130	| `lead-scanner` | `lda.duckdb` + lens name | candidate lead rows in ledger format | loader (+resolver for cross-dataset lenses) | lens SQL library runs standalone |
131	| `finding-verifier` | one finding file (§6 shape) + DB + raw data | verification block, verdict per claim | loader | agent workflow with scripted record fetches |
132	
133	Weak-skill risk flagged by QA: `investigation-ledger` and `lead-scanner` read as workflow wrappers unless script-backed. Mitigation: each ships runnable scripts (lint, lens SQL library); if the scoring-unit answer says per-skill scoring, merge them into the stronger skills at packaging.
134	
135	**Re-runnability contract (applies to every skill):**
136	- `DATA_ROOT` is a parameter everywhere; no hardcoded paths; `pathlib` throughout (we develop on Windows — evaluators likely run macOS/Linux).
137	- Pinned `requirements.txt` + stated Python version; DuckDB version pinned.
138	- **Cross-platform check:** each skill smoke-tested on Linux (WSL or container), not just Windows.
139	- **Clean-room test defined:** fresh clone + fresh venv + `pip install -r requirements.txt` + only the documented commands, on a non-dev environment (WSL/container). Loader + resolver clean-room at the **end of Phase 1**, not just Phase 5; all skills again at Phase 5.
140	- **Spec validation cadence:** pull the Agent Skills spec in Phase 0, scaffold all SKILL.md skeletons with required frontmatter then, validate at every phase boundary — not once at the end.
141	- No Market Trends references or jargon in any submitted artifact.
142	
143	---
144	
145	## 5. Traces
146	
147	- **Convention from the first working session:** where practical, one session = one skill-scoped unit of work. Session JSONL exported to `traces/YYYY-MM-DD_<skill-or-phase>_<lead-id>.jsonl`.
148	- **`traces/INDEX.md`:** finding → skill invocations → trace files → related `DECISIONS.md` entries. Built as we go; never reconstructed by archaeology at the end.
149	- **Render step:** small script converts JSONL → readable HTML (the brief allows "a rendered page"); panelists should not have to read raw JSONL.
150	- Capture everything now; the scrub-before-submission decision (paths, emails) is deferred but the capture policy is not.
151	
152	---
153	
154	## 6. Locked finding shape
155	
156	```
157	### Finding N — <sentence-case headline: phenomenon, verb, consequence>
158	
159	**What the records show:** 3–5 sentences, strictly what the filings/releases establish. No speculation.
160	
161	**Why it matters:** the reporter-chase test — the public-interest question this opens.
162	
163	**Caveats & alternative explanations:** self-reported data limits, innocent explanations,
164	what we could NOT verify.
165	
166	**Implicated parties & fairness note:** who is named; editorial-judgment check done
167	(innocent explanations weighed; naming proportionate to evidence).
168	
169	**Legal flag:** none | possible violation (cite the provision — e.g., LDA late/absent filing,
170	FARA non-registration) — flagged to the evaluation panel per the brief.
171	
172	**Verification:** re-derived by fresh agent on YYYY-MM-DD; verdicts per claim below;
173	trace: traces/<file>; decision log: DECISIONS.md#<entry>.
174	
175	— Sources: Senate filing_uuid <…> (what it evidences); house/<id>.xml (what it evidences);
176	  press release data/congress_press/<file>:<line> (what it evidences; url secondary);
177	  aggregate claims: SQL in queries/<file>.sql + rebuild via build_db.py + sampled records <ids>;
178	  external: <FEC/Congress.gov ref — disclosed in README>
179	```
180	
181	**"Locked" defined:** a finding is locked when its verification block passes — every claim independently re-derived — and the lock is logged in `DECISIONS.md`. Locked findings live one-per-file in `findings/`; the findings report is assembled **only** from locked findings.
182	
183	**Verifier protocol:** the fresh agent receives the finding's claims *with citation keys* but *without* the drafting session's reasoning. For each claim it must open the cited records itself (`show_record.py`) and re-derive the claim; for aggregates it re-runs the cited SQL. Verdict per claim: `verified` | `attribution error` (claim right, record wrong — re-cite) | `overstated` (substance right, framing oversells — hedge) | `fabricated` (drop). The per-claim tally stays in the verification block.
184	
185	**Headline discipline:** before lock, restate the headline next to the cumulative cited evidence — if the headline claims more than the records show, tighten it.
186	
187	---
188	
189	## 7. Workflow phases (timeboxed to the 2026-07-15 deadline)
190	
191	| Phase | Dates | Slip rule |
192	|---|---|---|
193	| 0 — Setup | Jul 4–5 | none available; repo already exists |
194	| 1 — Foundation + tracer bullet (pilot corpus) | Jul 5–8 | if data lands late, keep writing/unit-testing ETL against synthetic samples |
195	| 2 — Lead generation (pilot) | Jul 8–10 | run fewer lenses, prioritizing cross-dataset ones |
196	| 3 — Triage | Jul 10 | fixed — one sitting |
197	| 4 — Deep dives + verification | Jul 10–13 | cut the slate from 5 to 3 to 2; never cut verification |
198	| 5 — QA + packaging | Jul 13–15 | protected; submit morning of Jul 15 |
199	
200	**Phase 0 — Setup (now, while data downloads)**
201	- Git repo; README skeleton containing all six required elements verbatim (fill-in, not archaeology)
202	- Collect team conflict-of-interest statements (required README element)
203	- Trace convention tested end-to-end once (export a real session, name it, index it, render it)
204	- SKILL.md skeletons scaffolded from the Agent Skills spec; frontmatter validates
205	- `LEDGER.md`/`DECISIONS.md` templates created
206	- ETL scripts drafted against the manual's schemas; unit-tested on hand-built sample records
207	- Get the deadline; set the §2 timeboxes
208	
209	**Phase 1 — Data foundation + tracer bullet**
210	- ETL on real data; sanity counts vs the manual; fix schema surprises
211	- **Tracer bullet:** one lens, one slice, end-to-end to a draft finding + captured trace
212	- Press-enrichment cost measured on a one-month sample, then full deterministic run
213	- Entity resolution built to the depth the tracer bullet showed is needed
214	- Clean-room test: loader + resolver on WSL/container
215	- **Gate:** sanity report clean, tracer finding drafted, DB-engine decision confirmed (§8)
216	
217	**Phase 2 — Lead generation**
218	- All lenses run per the model-tier table; ledger populated (expect 20–50 leads)
219	- **Gate:** ledger lint passes; every lead has named actors or is marked accordingly
220	
221	**Phase 3 — Triage (human decision, logged)**
222	- Score: reporter-chase test, verifiability, effort. Named-actor rule enforced (aggregate-only leads auto-rejected or sent back for specificity)
223	- Slate of 3–5 locked; selections and kills logged in `DECISIONS.md`
224	
225	**Phase 4 — Deep investigation + verification**
226	- One session per lead; evidence chain with citation keys; external enrichment (Congress.gov / FEC / FARA / Federal Register) as needed, disclosed
227	- Verifier pass before each finding locks; mid-stream leads → ledger as `open`; triage checkpoint at phase end
228	
229	**Phase 5 — QA + packaging**
230	- **Cross-finding QA, three parallel auditors:** (1) *cite integrity* — open every citation and check it supports the claim; (2) *coverage* — anything verified in the ledger missing from the report, or report claims with no ledger trail; (3) *headline-vs-evidence* — does each headline overrun what its cited records cumulatively show
231	- Fairness + legal-flag editorial pass per the code of conduct
232	- All skills spec-validated and clean-room re-run; traces indexed and rendered; README completed against its skeleton

267	## 9. Tool roadmap — story-bucket-driven priorities (added 2026-07-06)
268	
269	**Input.** Teammate (Chris) contributed three *story buckets* the tools should serve plus three *data-integrity notes* (`../Story Buckets.md`). This section reprioritizes the tool build against those, scored by **how much each unlocks the data manual's own "starting points"** — the axis every competitor shares, so coverage there is table stakes and differentiation comes from packaging + correctness (see §2's caveat on the lens list).
270	
271	**Story buckets (the target outputs the tools exist to produce):**
272	1. **Regular synthesis** (beat tracking): Quarterly Turnover Tracker (who terminated/hired representation this quarter); Enterprise Map of an industry hidden under many terms (e.g. crypto) and vague categories (e.g. "taxation").
273	2. **Ad hoc** (rapid response): Press-Release Cross-Check (bill # → who's been quietly lobbying it); Who-Lobbies-the-White-House / executive branch; Breaking Nominee Audit (a new appointee's former clients' past disclosures).
274	3. **Investigative** (adversarial): Ghostwritten Policy Script (verbatim phrase reuse, corporate filings → politician press releases / bill text); favor-after-spend (spend burst → pardon/clemency/favorable action — already live as **L021** Daibes → "Executive relief").
275	
276	**The reframe: the three cleanup notes are correctness prerequisites, not features — and two are unaddressed.** They gate the accuracy of every bucket, and they point at the cheapest, highest-leverage work.
277	
278	| Cleanup note | Current build | Risk if unfixed |
279	|---|---|---|
280	| **A. Rollup double-count** — a client's own in-house registrant filing already covers the outside registrants that name it as client (AmEx ~$340k self-filed vs >$500k if summed); not universal, some clients roll up and some don't | **Not handled.** We dedup within a chamber and forbid summing Senate+House, but nothing detects a self/in-house rollup and prefers it over summing outside registrants | Inflates "biggest spender by client" — the top Senate starting point, and exactly the aggregate Stage-1 verification re-derives |
281	| **B. Generic free-text hides real activity** — "lobbying related to taxation" over a free-text that describes the actual asks | **Press side only** (`press_issue_mentions`); the lobbying free-text (`specific_issues/description`, Senate activity `description`) has only bill-number regex, no clustering | Blocks the Enterprise Map and honest issue aggregates |
282	| **C. Name inconsistency** across bills, orgs, and people (7-11 / 7-eleven / seven eleven) | **Partial.** Orgs/clients normalized in `lda-entity-resolver`; **bills** known-broken ("Farm Bill" vs H.R.# — killed L004); **people** not built at all | Leaks Nominee Audit, revolving door, Senate↔House reconciliation |

297	- **P4 jumps ahead of P3.** P4 is the *gate* for the Enterprise Map: it builds the comprehensive, entity-resolved industry player list from the lobbying free-text (crypto hidden under "taxation" etc.). P3 (turnover tracker) is a valuable recurring beat tool but is **not** on the crypto-map critical path, so it moves behind P4.
298	- **The money leg is already half-built.** The **LD-203 giving-map (a P7 slice) is built** (`ld203_giving.py` + `ld203_giving.sql`, 2026-07-07): feed it a roster → its disclosed LD-203 giving, recipients, per-entity split, citation keys. It answers "who they give money to" for *any* roster P4 produces.
299	- **FEC is now IN SCOPE, but sequenced behind P4** (and behind any name-resolution the list needs — P6 territory). FEC enriches the *player list* with the Super-PAC money LD-203 can't see (crypto's Fairshake); there is no point pulling FEC for a name-guessed roster, so it waits until P4 has produced the real, comprehensive list. It is the last required piece of the crypto money leg, not the first.

301	**Per-item rationale:**
302	- **P1 (BUILT 2026-07-06):** shipped as the `v_client_canonical_spend` view in `lda-entity-resolver` (cited demos + lead QA in `queries/p1_canonical_spend.sql`). Per (client, quarter), `canonical = greatest(in-house amount, outside amount)` — never their sum — with `has_inhouse_filing`, both components, the naive sum, the delta, and a `method` label all exposed for audit. It bridges the resolver's deliberate registrant/client split on `norm_key`. Two edge cases the naive idea misses and this handles: in-house filers that report under `income` not `expenses` (keying on `expenses` alone zeroed out real spenders like AIPAC), and clients that don't self-file at all. Validated on the full corpus: 2025 naive $6.23B vs canonical $5.46B (12.3% removed); L023 Vantive overstated ~$120–240k/qtr, L020/L024 unaffected. **We re-implemented the logic ourselves in Python/DuckDB** (a simple group-by-and-compare); lobbyR's `flag_client_registrant_conflict()`/`flag_dupes()` are cited as prior art + an independent cross-check, not copied — cleaner for licensing and the "novel capability" score. Disclose in README §4 with the COI note. P3/P4 inherit it.
303	- **P2 (BUILT 2026-07-07):** shipped as `skills/lead-scanner/scripts/bill_lookup.py` + `bill_aliases.json` (12-bill curated crosswalk) + `queries/p2_bill_crosscheck.sql`; lead-scanner de-skeletoned (empty-`scripts/` QA finding closed). Bill number **or** named alias → every Senate/House filing + press release touching it, each with a `show_record.py` key; number side = `bill_mentions`, name side = whole-word regex on press + filing free-text. Crosswalk keeps names vs match-phrases separate (ambiguous acronyms display-only) and leaves reused low numbers phrase-primary (no Congress field in `bill_mentions`; Farm Bill lives here). Demo: IRA press = 53 cite the number vs 3,157 name-only — the L004 say-vs-pay gap. Surfaced L028 (SECURE 2.0), then parked it as a known story after an outside-context-scan (contemporaneous/GDELT mode). Also added a per-year press facet + vintage caveat (press counts are window-biased: corpus starts 2022 and ~4x's by 2025).
304	- **P4 (◀ NEXT — the crypto Enterprise-Map gate):** extend the roadmap's FTS/BM25 + keyness discovery from press to the **lobbying** free-text so "taxation" resolves to specific asks (cleanup B), then emit an entity-resolved industry player list. Verified 2026-07-07 why this is the blocker: crypto free-text scatters across **12+ issue codes** (`senate_activities.description` 13,210 rows — FIN 3,504 / BAN 1,845 / TAX 1,110 / RET / HCR / ENV / SCI…, only ~27% under FIN; `house_alis.specific_issues` 12,014), the `ISSUE_KEYWORDS` tagger is **press-only** and folds crypto into a broad FIN bucket, and **no FTS index exists**. Serving layer stays deterministic/cited per the existing tagger pattern. Full kickoff brief below.
305	- **P3 (⏸ deferred — runs after P4):** the definitive Regular-synthesis beat tool — diff two quarters → new engagements, *terminated* ones, registrant swaps, in-house moves. Not on the crypto-map critical path, so bumped behind P4. **Method facts already verified (preserve for its eventual session):** termination is in the DB via senate `filing_type` T-family (`1T/2T/3T/4T`+`TY`, ~20.9k filings, display "…- Termination"; exact `termination_date` in the raw record but NOT loaded → optional `build_db.py` extension). Detect terminations from the T filing types **not** absence-between-quarters; Senate-primary (House `form` is only LD1/LD2); dedup on `filing_period` but treat a `T` as the terminal state so dedup doesn't hide it; dollar deltas via P1's `v_client_canonical_spend`; reuse `emergence_and_flows.sql` E1 (hires) + E2 (registrant-swap / in-house-move).
306	- **P5:** highest reporter-chase ceiling — verbatim shingle/near-dup matching, filings free-text → press (later bill) text. Novel but real engineering; gate behind P4 and the deadline.
307	- **P6:** the unstarted people-name half of cleanup C; also the name-resolution that tightens P4's player list and the LD-203 giving map (the Payward/Kraken split the giving-map's `--loose` mode currently patches over).
308	- **P7 (contribution/giving half BUILT 2026-07-07):** the **LD-203 giving-map** shipped as `skills/lead-scanner/scripts/ld203_giving.py` + `queries/ld203_giving.sql` (G1a–G1d) — an entity or roster → its disclosed LD-203 giving (totals, by contribution type, top recipients, per-entity split), each sample row a `show_record.py` `filing_uuid`. Resolves via the entity-resolver norm_key; dedups LD-203 amendments on the contribution-identity tuple (loader omits LD-203 `filing_type`, which the raw record carries — a future `build_db.py` extension). Two scope guards baked in: **attribution boundary** (LD-203 is registrant-filed → not attributable to an outside firm's clients; flags client-only queries) and **LD-203 ≠ FEC** (Super-PAC money out of scope → "disclosed LD-203 giving", never "total political spending"). Validated: Coinbase $1.70M (incl. $1M Trump-Vance inaugural; top members Lummis / French Hill); 12-name crypto roster ≈ $5.2M. Remaining P7: FEC enrichment (below) + the exec-branch targeting lens (L021 Daibes → clemency validates its shape).

338	
339	### P4 — kickoff brief (◀ NEXT session, cold-start ready)
340	
341	**Why this is next.** The active story is the **crypto Enterprise Map** (this section's own Story-Bucket-1 example): map an industry hidden under many terms and vague categories ("taxation"), showing who the players are, how much they spend, and who they give money to. The **money leg is already built** (the LD-203 giving-map, P7 slice — `ld203_giving.py`). What gates the story is the **comprehensive, entity-resolved industry player list**: you cannot map crypto by guessing names, because the players hide in the free-text across a dozen issue codes and diversified filers (Robinhood, PayPal, Fidelity, Stripe, banks) lobby on crypto without "crypto" in their name. P4 builds that list, which then feeds the money tools.
342	
343	**Goal.** A re-runnable **lobbying free-text discovery + clustering layer** that, given an industry seed (crypto first): (1) discovers the vocabulary the industry actually uses in the lobbying free-text; (2) tags that free-text to the vocabulary in a deterministic, cited serving table; (3) emits an **entity-resolved player list** (clients + registrants) that flows straight into the two money tools — `ld203_giving.py --names-file` (who they give to) and `v_client_canonical_spend` (how much they spend). Output = the Enterprise Map's who / how-much / who-they-give-to table, every row citation-keyed.
344	
345	**What already exists (verified 2026-07-07 on `db/lda_full.duckdb`).**
346	- **The problem, quantified.** Crypto free-text scatters across **12+ issue codes**: `senate_activities.description` has 13,210 crypto-lexicon rows — FIN 3,504 / BAN 1,845 / TAX 1,110 / RET 537 / HCR 478 / ENV / SCI / LBR / AGR / ENG / CPI / BUD… (only ~27% under FIN); `house_alis.specific_issues` 12,014. That spread is why issue-code filtering alone misses the industry.
347	- **The serving pattern to copy.** `ISSUE_KEYWORDS` in `build_db.py` → `press_issue_mentions` is the deterministic-tag pattern (tag → exact word + raw-record pointer, ~80% press coverage). It is **press-only** and folds crypto into a broad FIN bucket — P4 extends the pattern to the **lobbying** free-text and gives crypto its **own facet**.
348	- **No FTS index exists** (verified: zero `fts_main` schemas) — adding it is the cheapest first step.
349	- **Entity resolution is built** (`entities`/`entity_aliases`, norm_key); the money leg is built (`ld203_giving.py` + `queries/ld203_giving.sql`) and takes a `--names-file` roster — P4's output IS that roster, so the player list must be entity-resolved to plug into both money tools unchanged.
350	
351	**Build (discovery-vs-serving split, per `corpus_additions_roadmap.md` §1 — keep the serving layer deterministic/cited).**
352	1. **FTS/BM25 index over the lobbying free-text** (`senate_activities.description` + `house_alis.specific_issues`), in `build_db.py`'s index step (roadmap §1a). Turns "test a candidate crypto term" into a query, no rebuild; cheapest, no new deps; also unblocks P5.
353	2. **Discovery loop** (roadmap §1b, offline/reproducible): keyness/log-odds per issue code + quarterly emergence + untagged-set mining → a *ranked candidate-term list* for human triage. Reuse lobbyR's "broad Taxation query → text analysis" workflow + `whattheyresaying.csv` as a ground-truth sample (README §4 COI/prior-art disclosure).
354	3. **Curated crypto vocabulary file** — versioned, cited, same precision-over-recall discipline as `bill_aliases.json`; crypto gets its **own** tag, not folded into FIN.
355	4. **Serving table + script** — materialize a `lobbying_issue_mentions` (mirror of `press_issue_mentions`, raw-record pointers preserved) + a re-runnable script that emits the entity-resolved player list and feeds it to the money tools. Labeled `queries/p4_*.sql` block for the aggregate-cite rule.
356	
357	**Method decisions (the traps):**
358	1. **Serving stays deterministic and cited.** An LLM/embedding classifier may *discover* candidate terms but must never be the cited source of a finding — findings cite the keyword→exact-word→record chain (auditability is graded). Store any model-derived tag with a `method`/`model_version` column, separable from keyword tags.
359	2. **Recall is the whole point.** Name-`LIKE` on "crypto" misses diversified filers lobbying on crypto (Robinhood/PayPal/Fidelity/Stripe/banks). Derive the vocabulary from the free-text (what they *say* they lobby on), not from company names.
360	3. **Player list is entity-resolved** through `entities`/`entity_aliases` so it plugs into `ld203_giving.py` and `v_client_canonical_spend` unchanged — and so the giving-map's Payward/Kraken-style name splits (cleanup C / P6) stay a known, documented limitation, not a silent gap.
361	4. **Any spend rollup dedups on `filing_period`** (never `filing_type`) and uses P1's canonical spend, per repo CLAUDE.md "data facts."
362	5. **Named-actor rule.** "Crypto lobbying spend rose" is a dataset summary; the deliverable is named clients, their spend (P1), and their disclosed giving (LD-203 tool), each citation-keyed.
363	
364	**Then FEC (roadmap §3), sequenced AFTER P4 — now in scope.** Once P4 has produced the real, comprehensive, entity-resolved crypto player list, enrich its giving with the Super-PAC money LD-203 can't see (Fairshake): openFEC join keyed on the resolved entities, cache raw responses, report discrepancies. Needs an `api.data.gov` key + README §4 disclosure. **Do NOT pull FEC before P4** — enriching a name-guessed roster is wasted work; FEC is the last required piece of the money leg, not the first.
365	
366	**Acceptance.** For crypto: the discovery layer surfaces the scattered issue codes + a candidate vocabulary a human triages into the crypto facet; the serving table tags the lobbying free-text with raw-record pointers; the emitted entity-resolved player list, fed to `ld203_giving.py --names-file` and `v_client_canonical_spend`, produces a who / how-much / who-they-give-to table with `show_record.py`-resolvable keys. Spot-check: a diversified filer caught by free-text but NOT by name-`LIKE` (proves recall); 3 players round-trip through both money tools. Ledger the map as a candidate Enterprise-Map lead (builds on L029).
367	
368	**Workflow.** Directly on `main`, solo writer, one scoped session; start by reading the repo CLAUDE.md + `LEDGER.md`/`DECISIONS.md` tail (esp. L029) + this §9. DB is `db/lda_full.duckdb`; raw tree is at `../data/data` (`show_record.py --data-root ../data/data`). End: export the session trace, update `traces/INDEX.md`, commit + push.
369	
370	---
371	
372	## Appendix A — Lineage (internal only)
373	
374	The plan adapts Rob's Market Trends Research process (`Sample Process/market-trends-research-main/`). Condensed mapping, for the team's orientation only — **none of these terms or files may appear in submitted artifacts**:
375	
376	- Deterministic derived artifacts (index/scan generators) → Layer 1 ETL + aggregates
377	- Navigation vs deep-read role separation (scanners return filenames, never quotes) → lens scanners return record IDs only; deep-read agents cite raw records
378	- file:line citation rule + "never cite derived artifacts" → citation keys + "never cite the database"
379	- Pre-lock audit agents + fabrication taxonomy → `finding-verifier` protocol (§6)
380	- Locked-theme entry shape → locked finding shape (§6)
381	- Triple-auditor cross-theme QA (integrity/coverage/synopsis) → Phase 5 cross-finding QA (§7)
382	- Validation gates with locked sets → triage gate + slate lock, softened with mid-stream ledger intake (§3 Layer 2)
